abstract: Ensure that dates, currencies, and numbers display correctly across multiple languages and locales by using formatters.
overview:
## Overview

Languages and regions have different formats for presenting dates and numbers. Some languages use the period (.) as a decimal separator, and others use the comma (,). Some place the percent sign before the number when formatting percentages. And many regions display time and date differently, despite being part of the same language.

Instead of trying to account for all these variations yourself, use the formatters built into Foundation to create localizable versions of the dates and numbers you want to present.

### Format dates with predefined styles

To convert a date or number into a localizable string, use the Foundation formatters and styles. These APIs take instances of your date and number objects, and convert them into localizable formatted strings according to the locale of the device your app is running on.

For example, to create a localizable string from a date object, create an instance of the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Date] you want to format and then call the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Date/formatted()] function on the date.

To vary the date components that display, or display only the time or the date, use the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Date/formatted(date:time:)] method on the `Date` object passing in instances of [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Date/FormatStyle/DateStyle] and [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Date/FormatStyle/TimeStyle].

### Create your own custom date styles

To format a date to a specific style, create your own custom date style including only the date properties you want to display.

For example, to create a date that includes only the month, day, and year:

You can also achieve the same result in one line.

The order of the fields you pass into the `formatted(_:)` function doesn’t matter. For example, these lines of code produce the same result.

Customize the date styles you want to display by chaining together instances of [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Date/FormatStyle/Symbol] structures along with their respective formatting properties.

### Format percents and scientific numbers

If you want to create a localizable string for a number (such as [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/Int], [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/Double], [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Decimal], or [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/Float]), call `formatted()` or `formatted(_:)` on the number instance, along with the format style to display.

For example, to create a formatted version of an `Int`, call the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/BinaryInteger/formatted()] function on the number.

To format the number as a percent, call `formatted(_ format:)` on the number you want to display with the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/NumberFormatter/Style/percent] number format style. Integers convert directly into percentages using the whole number.

Fractions convert between the range of 0 and 1.

To display a number using scientific notation, call [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/Sequence/formatted(_:)] on the number to display using the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/NumberFormatStyleConfiguration/Notation/scientific], [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/FloatingPointFormatStyle/notation(_:)], and [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/FormatStyle/number-3luf2] format styles.

### Format times as intervals or durations

To display an interval of time as a localizable string:

To display time as a duration, you can similarly define a date range and convert that range into a duration.

You can also use factory methods like [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/Duration/seconds(_:)-5ifzr] on the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/Duration] structure to produce localizable durations from a single number.

For example, to display a given number of seconds as a duration of time:

### Format items as lists

To create a localizable string in the form of a list, use the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/ListFormatStyle] structure along with either the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/Sequence/formatted()] or [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/Sequence/formatted(_:)] function to create a string representation of a [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/Sequence] of items.

You can also create lists using different formatting styles by calling the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/FormatStyle/list(memberStyle:type:width:)] function along with specific list format styles.

### Convert and display measurement units across different locales

Units of measure vary significantly depending on the locale the format style uses. For example, a distance in feet for the `en_US` locale appears as meters using the French locale `fr_FR`.

To ensure your units of measure convert and display properly across different languages and regions:

For example, say you want to convert and display the following measurements.

To convert them using the default format, call [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Measurement/formatted(_:)] on the measurement object.

To customize the output, call the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Measurement/formatted(_:)] function on the measurement using the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/FormatStyle/measurement(width:usage:hidesScaleName:numberFormatStyle:)] factory method to create the format and style you want.

### Format dates and numbers in SwiftUI

To format dates and numbers in SwiftUI, use the `format` initializers on SwiftUI view controls to customize how those strings display.

For example, here is a SwiftUI view that displays three different localizable formats of `Date` using the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/SwiftUI/Text/init(_:format:)] initializer from the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/SwiftUI/Text] view.

This example uses the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/SwiftUI/TextField/init(_:value:format:prompt:)-7flsn] initializer of the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/SwiftUI/TextField] view to present a number as a percentage for a tip.

## Test the formatters

To test and see how your formatters display in different languages and regions, create an instance of a [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/Locale] object, passing in the `identifier` of the region you want to test. Then set that locale on the output of your formatted string to see how that string displays in that language and region.

For example, you can see how your localizable strings display in French as follows.

To test your formatters in SwiftUI, set the locale in the environment variable in the `#Preview` section of your code.

items.description: Find text in your app that needs translation and verify that your interface adapts to translated text.
items.name: Preparing your interface for localization

items.description: Make your app’s text translatable by leveraging the localization APIs in the Foundation framework.
items.name: Preparing your app’s text for translation

title: Preparing dates, currencies, and numbers for translation
